weather: Today in Edmonds: Overcast morning giving way to mostly sunny afternoon. High 59-62°F, light south winds 10-15 mph. Rain chance only 2-20%. Tonight turns partly cloudy then cloudy after midnight, low 47-49°F, south breeze 5-10 mph. Pleasant spring day overall.

local_news.findings: The City of Edmonds will begin its 2025 Combined Cured-in-Place Pipe (CIPP) Rehabilitation Project on May 4, 2026, to repair aging sewer and stormwater infrastructure across the city. This project addresses critical infrastructure needs identified in previous assessments.

Edmonds is at the center of redistricting discussions as South County Fire commissioners work to incorporate the city's 42,000+ residents into the Regional Fire Authority's governance structure. Two competing plans are under consideration - one keeping Edmonds whole in a single district, another splitting the city along Pine Street. Public input is being sought before final adoption.

A 58-year-old woman was found unresponsive in her cell at the Snohomish County Jail in Everett during routine morning checks. The incident occurred around 7:25 a.m. on Wednesday, April 22, 2026. The death is under investigation by the Snohomish County Medical Examiner's Office.

career.notes: Most postings are pool or general listings for 2026-27; exact building assignments released later. Apply early—many districts review on rolling basis.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica holds annual auditions every August for all voice parts. Rehearsals are Tuesday evenings 6:30–9:30 pm at Epiphany Parish (1805 38th Ave, Seattle). Requirements: vocalizing, sight-reading two short choral pieces with a quartet, repeating a phrase by ear, and preparing a 2-minute solo plus three provided excerpts. Email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the 2026-27 mailing list. Seattle Choral Company schedules auditions year-round; submit an application and, if invited, attend a 30-minute session at their studio where you’ll sing two unaccompanied choral excerpts, do vocal exercises, and read passages in five languages. Choral Arts Northwest is hosting an Educator Sing-in on March 15, 2026, at First United Methodist Church—no audition required, clock hours provided.  [TRUNCATED]
family.toddler: Edmonds Library, 650 Main St, offers Toddler Storytime for ages 18–36 months on Mondays at 10:30 am and Thursdays at 9:30 am (next sessions April 27 & 30). Baby Storytime (0–18 mo) runs Tuesdays 9:00 & 10:00 am. All free, no registration. Imagine Children’s Museum, 1502 Wall St, Everett, has SPLAT! sensory play for 2–5-year-olds on the 2nd floor Art Hangout plus daily hands-on STEM exhibits; open 10 am–5 pm Tue–Sun, $12 adults/$10 toddlers. Woodland Park Zoo’s new Forest Trailhead opens May 1 with stroller-friendly treetop paths, red pandas, and tree kangaroos—perfect for toddlers. Sno-Isle Libraries system also lists Little Science Lab pop-ups with the museum at Lynnwood Library (next April 27, 2–2:30 pm).

global_news.findings: North Korea has confirmed for the first time that it sent 14,000 troops to fight for Russia in Ukraine under Kim Jong Un's direct orders, marking an unprecedented escalation in the conflict. Russian President Vladimir Putin praised the North Korean forces as 'heroes' who helped expel Ukrainian forces from the Kursk region. The US State Department and South Korea condemned the deployment as illegal and a violation of UN resolutions.

Iran has offered to reopen the Strait of Hormuz in exchange for the US lifting its blockade on Iranian ports and ending the war, according to two regional officials. The standoff continues at this critical waterway through which 20% of global oil and natural gas passes, with ships currently waiting as Iran restricts movement and the US enforces its naval blockade.

New details have emerged about the shooting at the White House Correspondents' Dinner, where a suspect allegedly attempted to assassinate President Trump. The incident has sent shockwaves through Washington as investigators piece together how the attack unfolded during the high-profile event.

UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer faces a parliamentary vote tomorrow on whether to launch an inquiry into his handling of Peter Mandelson's appointment. The controversy centers on whether Starmer misled Parliament about the vetting process for Mandelson, who was fired last September over his ties to Jeffrey Epstein.

British police seized £175,000 worth of gold and silver bullion along with £13,000 cash during an anti-slavery raid in Hull. A 35-year-old man was arrested on suspicion of slavery offenses and money laundering, highlighting ongoing efforts to combat human trafficking in the UK.

intellectual_journals.findings: Jake Nevins examines how the 2026 Winter Olympics in Italy became a stage for geopolitical theater, contrasting the IOC's hollow calls for "armonia" with the reality of escalating US-Iran conflict. The piece traces how American athletes became unwitting propaganda tools while the Olympic Truce proved meaningless as Operation Epic Fury commenced just days after the games closed.

Colm Tóibín analyzes the complex path to potential Irish reunification through Fintan O'Toole and Sam McBride's book 'For and Against a United Ireland'. The review explores demographic shifts, economic disparities, and the practical challenges of merging two health systems and public sectors, noting that Catholics now outnumber Protestants in Northern Ireland for the first time.

Flora Champy's essay challenges both conservative and progressive moralizing about literature, arguing instead that reading's true value lies in cultivating our capacity for truth-seeking rather than delivering predetermined lessons. Drawing on Proust's disagreement with Ruskin, she positions literature as a space for freedom rather than moral instruction.

Christian Lorentzen reviews Ben Lerner's 'Transcription', finding it a meditation on middle age, mortality, and artistic method. The novel explores generational transmission through the relationship between a poet narrator and his aging mentor Thomas, using broken phones and glass flowers as metaphors for how we record and distort the past.

Liam Shaw traces the history of insulin from 19th-century glandular experiments to modern pharmaceutical oligopoly, revealing how three companies maintain global dominance through patent manipulation and price-fixing. The piece connects the 1920s Toronto discovery to contemporary American healthcare failures, where insulin rationing has become a leading cause of death for diabetics.

triadic_ontology.findings: The Pattern Operator: How Relational Structure Is Generated, Bounded, and Revealed by Denis Bailey (March 2026) presents a unified operator-level account that identifies a single relational mechanism underlying the emergence, stability, and intelligibility of reality. Bailey argues that the same operator manifests in three irreducible modes—generation, boundary, and revelation—which constitute the minimal conditions for any relational structure to exist. In physics, this manifests as light: the generator of electromagnetic structure, the boundary defining causal gradients, and the medium making information observable. In metaphysics, it corresponds to the ground of being: source of form, sustainer of order, and revealer of meaning. The paper demonstrates structural identity between this triadic operator and the Christian doctrine of the Trinity, mapping Father to generation, Son to boundary/form, and Spirit to revelation. This provides a coherent, scale-invariant model linking physics, [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: The autonomous research landscape has reached a watershed moment. Google DeepMind's Deep Research Max, powered by Gemini 3.1 Pro, now orchestrates full end-to-end scientific workflows—from literature review to manuscript submission—via a dual-agent architecture that balances speed and depth. In parallel, the open-source Mimosa framework demonstrates 43.1% success on ScienceAgentBench by evolving task-specific multi-agent topologies through experimental feedback loops. MIT's SCIENCECLAW+INFINITE system takes a radically decentralized approach: 300+ interoperable skills, immutable artifact DAGs, and emergent peer-to-peer convergence among independent agents. Sakana AI's commercial Fugu platform operationalizes these insights, coordinating frontier models via collective intelligence. Meanwhile, Google's PaperOrchestra automates the entire paper-writing pipeline—outline, plotting, literature review, and peer-review simulation—achieving 84-81% simulated acceptance rates at CVPR/ICLR. These  [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: President Trump has directed the Pentagon to declassify and release all government files on UFOs, UAPs, and non-human intelligence, triggering the quiet registration of aliens.gov by the Executive Office of the President on March 18, 2026. While the Pentagon’s All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) reports over 2,000 documented UAP cases, it maintains there is “no evidence” of extraterrestrial contact. Congressional momentum is building: Rep. Tim Burchett, after a classified briefing, said its contents “would set the Earth on fire,” and Rep. Anna Paulina Luna has formally demanded release of 46 military UAP videos. Former intelligence officials David Grusch and Luis Elizondo reiterated under oath that crash-retrieval programs and “non-human biologics” exist, though no timeline for disclosure has been confirmed. Skeptics caution that most classified material is withheld to protect sources and methods rather than proof of aliens.

newyorker.title: The New Yorker
newyorker.dek: In the wake of the White House Correspondents’ Dinner shooting, the press corps reconsiders its relationship to power.
newyorker.text:
The annual White House Correspondents’ Dinner, held this year at the Washington Hilton, was supposed to be a return to normalcy—a glitzy, self-congratulatory affair where journalists and politicians mingled over rubber chicken and weak jokes. Instead, it ended in chaos when shots rang out inside the ballroom, sending President Trump and hundreds of guests scrambling for cover. No one was harmed, and the Secret Service quickly secured the president, but the incident has left the press corps rattled and rethinking its role in an era of heightened political violence.

The dinner, often criticized as a symbol of the media’s coziness with power, had already been under scrutiny. This year, several news organizations had scaled back their presence, citing ethical concerns and the administration’s hostility toward the press. But the shooting—details of which remain murky, though authorities have ruled out terrorism—has accelerated a reckoning. Some journalists who attended described a surreal scene: tuxedos and gowns abandoned on the floor, reporters ducking under tables, and the president being rushed out through a kitchen exit.

In the aftermath, the White House Correspondents’ Association issued a statement condemning violence and reaffirming the press’s commitment to "fearless reporting." But privately, many journalists are questioning whether the dinner should continue at all. "It’s hard to celebrate the First Amendment when you’re hiding behind a dessert cart," one reporter quipped.

The incident has also reignited debates about access journalism and the media’s role in legitimizing power. Critics argue that events like the dinner blur the line between reporting and reveling, while defenders say they’re a necessary evil in a town where relationships matter. Either way, the shooting has made one thing clear: the old rituals of Washington may no longer be sustainable in a country where political violence feels increasingly routine.

enriched_articles.title: Edmonds to begin sewer, storm pipe rehabilitation project May 4
enriched_articles.text: The City of Edmonds will begin a sewer and storm pipe rehabilitation project starting Monday, May 4, 2026. The project will focus on aging infrastructure in the downtown area and is expected to last approximately six weeks. City officials say the work will involve trenchless pipe rehabilitation technology to minimize disruption to local businesses and residents. The project is part of a larger multi-year effort to upgrade Edmonds' aging water infrastructure, with an estimated cost of $2.3 million funded through state grants and municipal bonds. Traffic impacts will be minimal as most work will occur underground, though some parking restrictions may apply on Main Street and 3rd Avenue South during the first phase of construction.

enriched_articles.title: Edmonds at center of discussions as South County Fire commissioners embark on redistricting
enriched_articles.text: Edmonds has emerged as a focal point in ongoing discussions about redistricting for South County Fire & Rescue services. Fire commissioners are considering boundary changes that could significantly impact how emergency services are allocated across the region. The proposed changes would potentially shift some areas currently served by Edmonds Fire Department to neighboring districts, while bringing other areas under Edmonds' jurisdiction. Public hearings are scheduled for May 12 and May 19 at the Edmonds City Hall, where residents can provide input on the proposed boundary adjustments. The redistricting process, which occurs every ten years, aims to balance population growth with emergency response times across South Snohomish County.

enriched_articles.title: Woman, 58, died in her cell at Snohomish County Jail
enriched_articles.text: An inmate died inside her cell Wednesday morning at Snohomish County Jail. Around 7:25 a.m. Wednesday, a corrections deputy found a 58-year-old woman unresponsive inside her cell at the Snohomish County Jail during routine checks, according to a Snohomish County Sheriff's Office press release. She was housed in the jail's medical housing unit. Corrections staff declared a medical emergency and began lifesaving measures, but resuscitation attempts were unsuccessful, the release said. The Everett Police Department booked the woman into the Snohomish County Jail on April 20 for suspicion of loitering, possession of a controlled substance and a Stay Out of Drug Areas (SODA) violation. Following her death, an unexpected fatality review will occur, the release said, which is a review of the death from a clinical standpoint. The Sheriff's Office Major Crimes Unit will conduct a separate death investigation. Snohomish County Medical Examiner's Office will determine the identity of the woman as well as the cause and manner of death, the release said.

enriched_articles.title: North Korea confirms troop deployment to Russia for first time, KCNA reports
enriched_articles.text: North Korea has confirmed it has deployed troops to Russia for the first time, state media KCNA reported on Sunday, marking a significant escalation in military cooperation between the two countries. The deployment includes an unspecified number of North Korean military personnel who will participate in what KCNA described as 'joint military exercises and training operations' with Russian forces. The move comes amid deepening military ties between Pyongyang and Moscow, with North Korea providing artillery shells and missiles to support Russia's war effort in Ukraine. South Korea's defense ministry expressed 'grave concern' over the deployment, warning it could destabilize regional security. The United States called the deployment 'a dangerous expansion of the DPRK-Russia military partnership' and said it would coordinate with allies on an appropriate response. This marks the first time North Korea has officially acknowledged sending military personnel to Russia since the Ukraine war began.

enriched_articles.title: 'Sense of disbelief': how the White House press dinner attack unfolded
enriched_articles.text: White House correspondents and guests have described their 'sense of disbelief' as a shooting incident unfolded during the White House Correspondents' Dinner on Saturday night. The attack occurred as President Trump was delivering remarks at the Washington Hilton hotel, when shots were fired inside the ballroom. Secret Service agents immediately rushed the president to safety, while attendees scrambled for cover. Witnesses reported hearing multiple gunshots before security personnel secured the area. The incident prompted an immediate lockdown of the hotel and surrounding areas. No fatalities were reported, though several people sustained minor injuries during the evacuation. The White House Correspondents' Association later confirmed that the dinner would be rescheduled, while law enforcement agencies launched a comprehensive investigation into the security breach.
